macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Abaye-Rava Debate on Effectiveness of Prohibited Acts

micro_segments.title: Setting up the fundamental question: Does a prohibited act take halachic effect?
Abaye's position: Acts in violation still take effect (im avid mahani)
Rava's position: Acts in violation do not take effect (lo mahani)

macro_segments.title: The Case of the Rapist Divorcing His Wife: Examining the Obligation to Remarry

micro_segments.title: The mishna on ones shegeirash: rapist divorces and remarries
First version: understanding the case through the lens of effective vs. ineffective divorce
Rava's response: the obligation to remarry counteracts the lashes through a positive mitzvah
Second version (lishna achrina): focusing on kol yamav and the obligation to reverse the divorce
Abaye's explanation: the word kol yamav creates an obligation, not just an option
Rava's explanation: kol yamav comes to distinguish this case from the general principle

macro_segments.title: The Case of Taking Terumah from Bad Grain Upon Good Grain

micro_segments.title: The mishna: taking terumah from inferior grain upon superior grain is prohibited but effective
Challenge to Rava: this case shows terumah works even when done improperly
Rava's exception via Rabbi Ilai: special drasha shows this case had to work
Abaye's use of the drasha: the emphasis on sin teaches it is indeed a sin

macro_segments.title: The Case of Taking Terumah from One Species Upon Another Species

micro_segments.title: The mishna: taking from one produce species to another does not work (ein trumato truma)
Challenge to Abaye: this case shows terumah doesn't work when species are mixed
Abaye's exception: the verse reishitam tells us each species must be separate
Rava's use of the same verse: emphasizing the need for separation due to the joined language

macro_segments.title: The Case of Redeeming Consecrated Animals: Charamim, Bechor, and Maaser

micro_segments.title: Charamim that belong to kohanim: cannot be redeemed and the redemption is ineffective
Abaye's exception: the word hu emphasizes the status remains unchanged
Rava's explanation of hu: limiting the rule that items cannot be sold to only charamim and maaser
Bechor: cannot be redeemed; the word hem applies to bechor and maaser
Abaye's use of hem: emphasizing the status remains with the original animal
Rava's use of hem: teaching that blood does not nullify one another on the altar

macro_segments.title: The Case of Redeeming Consecrated Animals: Charamim, Bechor, and Maaser (continued)

micro_segments.title: Maaser: like bechor, cannot be redeemed; the rule derived from bechor

macro_segments.title: The Case of Taking Terumah and Maaser Out of Order

micro_segments.title: The mishna: taking terumah before bikkurim or maaser before terumah violates but is effective
The language of the mishna: explicitly states 'even though it's forbidden, what was done is done'
Challenge to Rava: this case shows the act takes effect despite violation
Rava's exception: the verse mikol matnoteichem tells us when terumah must be taken
Abaye's need for the verse: limiting the obligation to when the obligation has kicked in
Rav Pappa's challenge to Abaye: questioning the boundary of when the obligation applies
Abaye's response: the obligation only applies once the grain has been piled and processed

macro_segments.title: The Case of a Kohen Gadol Marrying a Widow

micro_segments.title: The halacha: kiddushin takes effect when a kohen gadol marries a widow despite the prohibition
Challenge to Rava: the braisa explicitly states there is kiddushin in this case
Rava's exception: the verse lo yechalel zaroh tells us the act is effective
Abaye's use of lo yechalel zaroh: teaching that both child and woman are affected by the union

topical_tags.term: im avid mahani (if done, it takes effect)
bad grain on good grain (min hara al hayafa)
vehaya hu utemurato yihyeh kodesh (both become holy)
lo yechalel zaroh (shall not profane seed)
mikol matnoteichem tarimu (from all your gifts take)
ain olim mevatlin zeh et zeh (offerings don't negate each other)
